prompt:
A political scientist argues that the coalition’s success was <u>contingent</u> on support from regional parties that could withdraw at any moment.
As used in the text, what does “contingent” most nearly mean?

choices.A: decorative
choices.B: dependent
choices.C: surprising
choices.D: brief

explanation: “Contingent on” means dependent on or conditioned by something else.

prompt:
A historian argues that wall-length timelines in museums do more than arrange events in order. By deciding where periods begin and end and which developments appear continuous or broken, a timeline can guide visitors toward particular ideas about cause, momentum, and historical change. Thus, chronology itself can be an interpretive framework, not just a neutral container.
Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.A: Wall-length timelines in museums can shape interpretation by suggesting particular patterns of change and causation.
choices.B: Museum timelines are useful only when they cover a very short period.
choices.C: Visitors pay attention to timelines mainly because they include bright colors.
choices.D: Historical change can be understood only through objects, not chronology.

explanation: The passage argues that timelines influence how visitors think about historical change, not just order events.

prompt:
Legal historians studying merchant disputes often use court dockets because they preserve dates, names, and monetary claims. Yet these records capture only conflicts that reached formal hearing; private settlements and informal mediation rarely appear. As a result, docket books may exaggerate how often commercial disagreements ended in court.
Which choice can most reasonably be inferred from the text?

choices.A: Court dockets may make formal litigation seem more common than it actually was in commercial disputes.
choices.B: Private settlements were always recorded more carefully than lawsuits.
choices.C: Merchant disputes rarely involved money.
choices.D: Legal historians can reconstruct informal mediation directly from docket books.

explanation: Because private settlements and informal mediation rarely appear, dockets can overemphasize formal court resolution.

prompt:
A literary critic notes that an essay repeatedly describes scientific experiments as rehearsals rather than discoveries. The critic argues that this language emphasizes preparation, revision, and uncertainty over sudden revelation.
Which choice best describes the function of the first sentence in the text?

choices.A: It introduces a repeated metaphor that the second sentence interprets.
choices.B: It summarizes the results of the experiments described in the essay.
choices.C: It argues that the essay should avoid figurative language.
choices.D: It explains why the essay was published before the research was complete.

explanation: The first sentence identifies the metaphor of experiments as rehearsals, and the second explains what that metaphor emphasizes.

prompt:
A student is writing about mycelium packaging and has taken these notes:
• Mycelium packaging is grown from fungal fibers and agricultural waste.
• It can replace some foam packaging.
• It is biodegradable.
• Performance varies with moisture and storage conditions.
• Large-scale production remains a challenge.
The student wants to emphasize both a potential advantage of mycelium packaging and a challenge facing wider adoption. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.A: Mycelium packaging can replace some foam and is biodegradable, but its performance and large-scale production still present challenges.
choices.B: Foam packaging is produced only from agricultural waste.
choices.C: Mycelium packaging is unaffected by moisture and storage conditions.
choices.D: Large-scale production guarantees better performance.

explanation: This choice includes both the environmental advantage and the practical limits described in the notes.

prompt:
The linguist expected the newly found letters to confirm that the dialect had disappeared by 1900. ______, several late letters show speakers still using distinctive verb forms in the 1920s.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?

choices.A: Likewise,
choices.B: Instead,
choices.C: For example,
choices.D: Therefore,

explanation: The letters did not confirm disappearance by 1900; they showed continued use in the 1920s instead.

prompt:
The manuscript <u>—copied decades after the events it describes—</u> cannot be treated as a simple eyewitness record.
Which choice best punctuates the underlined portion of the text?

choices.A: NO CHANGE
choices.B: , copied decades after the events it describes,
choices.C: ; copied decades after the events it describes;
choices.D: : copied decades after the events it describes:

explanation: The dashes correctly set off the nonessential phrase about when the manuscript was copied.

prompt:
Although often framed as pure entertainment, the annual parade also served as a public claim to visibility by communities that were usually absent from official ceremonies.
Which choice best describes the function of the introductory phrase in the text?

choices.A: It contrasts a common description of the parade with a broader explanation of its social meaning.
choices.B: It identifies the first year in which the parade was held.
choices.C: It explains how the parade route was chosen.
choices.D: It presents the results of a survey about parade attendance.

explanation: The phrase “Although often framed as pure entertainment” sets up a contrast with the parade’s deeper role.

prompt:
After Elena spoke with Marcus about Priya’s proposal, <u>she</u> recommended postponing the vote.
Which choice best revises the text to avoid ambiguity?

choices.A: she
choices.B: Elena
choices.C: they
choices.D: someone

explanation: Replacing “she” with “Elena” removes the ambiguity among the named people.

prompt:
Had the researchers calibrated the cameras before the eclipse, they <u>know</u> by now whether the dim streaks were caused by dust or by the lens itself.
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: know
choices.B: knew
choices.C: would know
choices.D: would have known

explanation: The sentence describes an unreal past condition with a present result, so “would know” is correct.

prompt:
The editor meant to say that the archive preserved letters primarily from coastal towns, but the sentence as drafted says that the archive <u>primarily preserved letters from coastal towns and shipping records from inland markets.</u>
Which choice best revises the text so that “primarily” is placed as precisely as possible?

choices.A: primarily preserved letters from coastal towns and shipping records from inland markets.
choices.B: preserved primarily letters from coastal towns and shipping records from inland markets.
choices.C: preserved letters primarily from coastal towns and shipping records from inland markets.
choices.D: preserved letters from coastal towns and shipping records primarily from inland markets.

explanation: This placement makes clear that “primarily” modifies the source of the letters, not the verb “preserved.”

prompt:
[1] In floodplain forests, seasonal flooding carries nutrients and seeds across wide areas. [2] When levees block that movement, some parts of the forest receive less fresh sediment and fewer new seedlings. [3] Restoration projects may therefore reopen side channels or remove barriers so water can spread more naturally. [4] These projects aim to restore not only water flow but also the ecological processes tied to it.
Which choice best describes the most logical placement for the following sentence?
“Reconnecting the floodplain can change both what materials arrive and where young plants can take hold.”

choices.A: Before sentence 1
choices.B: After sentence 2
choices.C: After sentence 3
choices.D: After sentence 4

explanation: The added sentence explains the ecological consequence of blocked flood movement before the paragraph turns to restoration.

prompt:
A researcher compared the average number of minutes four groups spent discussing a case study.
Group A: 26
Group B: 23
Group C: 19
Group D: 21
A student claims that Group C spent the least time discussing the case study, on average. Which choice best evaluates the student’s claim?

choices.A: The claim is supported because Group C’s average is lower than those of the other groups.
choices.B: The claim is unsupported because Group B’s average is also above twenty minutes.
choices.C: The claim is unsupported because Group D discussed the case for nearly as long as Group B.
choices.D: The claim is supported because Group C’s average is greater than Group D’s.

explanation: Group C’s 19 minutes is less than Group A’s 26, Group B’s 23, and Group D’s 21.

prompt:
In discussing the mural, the critic insists that the painted birds are not <u>incidental</u>; they guide the viewer’s eye from the harbor to the crowd on the steps.
As used in the text, what does “incidental” most nearly mean?

choices.A: decorative
choices.B: accidental
choices.C: minor
choices.D: ancient

explanation: In context, “incidental” means secondary or minor, not central.

prompt:
In a novel, the narrator says, “My grandmother kept the guest room ready even after the last relatives stopped visiting; the bed was made, and a glass pitcher was replaced each Monday. The room seemed less prepared for arrival than unwilling to admit absence.”
Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.A: The grandmother keeps the room ready as a quiet refusal to accept lasting absence.
choices.B: The narrator plans to turn the room into a study.
choices.C: The relatives continue visiting every weekend.
choices.D: The room is maintained only because it is rented to guests.

explanation: The room’s careful upkeep suggests resistance to accepting that visitors are gone for good.

prompt:
A study found that students who arranged cards showing the stages of a cell process in their own order later recalled the sequence more accurately than students who reread the same stages in list form. The researchers caution, however, that the study did not test whether arranging the cards improved recall of specialized vocabulary.
Which choice can most reasonably be inferred from the text?

choices.A: Arranging stages physically may help students remember sequence even if its effect on technical terms is uncertain.
choices.B: Rereading list form is never useful for learning science.
choices.C: Specialized vocabulary is always easier to remember than sequence.
choices.D: The study proved that arranging cards is the best strategy for every science topic.

explanation: The study found better recall of sequence but did not test specialized vocabulary.

prompt:
A historian argues that early tide tables gained public authority partly because merchants could use them to coordinate departures and deliveries. To support this claim, the historian notes that rival newspapers often copied the tables into their own pages even while criticizing the editor’s politics.
Why does the author most likely include the example of rival newspapers copying the tables?

choices.A: To show that the tables’ practical usefulness made them valuable even to opponents
choices.B: To prove that rival newspapers wrote the original tables
choices.C: To argue that editors cared more about politics than about shipping
choices.D: To suggest that all merchants read the same newspaper

explanation: Rival newspapers copied the tables despite political criticism, suggesting the tables were useful and authoritative.

prompt:
A student is writing about floating wetlands and has taken these notes:
• Floating wetlands use buoyant mats planted with wetland species.
• Plant roots hang into the water and can absorb some nutrients.
• These systems may improve water quality in ponds or canals.
• Their effectiveness depends on maintenance and local water conditions.
The student wants to explain a potential benefit of floating wetlands while acknowledging a practical limitation. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.A: Floating wetlands can help improve water quality by using plant roots to absorb nutrients, but their effectiveness depends on upkeep and site conditions.
choices.B: Wetland species can grow only on natural shorelines.
choices.C: Ponds and canals never need water-quality monitoring once plants are added.
choices.D: Floating mats work equally well in all water conditions.

explanation: This choice includes both the potential benefit and the practical limitation from the notes.

prompt:
The scholars agreed that the inscription was badly damaged. ______, they disagreed about whether the missing lines should be restored digitally or left unread.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?

choices.A: Consequently,
choices.B: Nevertheless,
choices.C: Similarly,
choices.D: For example,

explanation: The second sentence contrasts agreement about the damage with disagreement about how to respond to it.

prompt:
The seminar asks students to compare versions, annotate evidence, and <u>the revision of their claims.</u>
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: the revision of their claims.
choices.B: revising their claims.
choices.C: revise their claims.
choices.D: to revising their claims.

explanation: “Revise” matches the parallel verbs “compare” and “annotate.”

prompt:
The final chapter, <u>written after the author had moved abroad,</u> reflects a more skeptical view of the city.
Which choice best describes the underlined portion of the sentence?

choices.A: It is a nonessential phrase correctly set off with commas.
choices.B: It is a restrictive phrase that should not be set off with commas.
choices.C: It creates a sentence fragment.
choices.D: It should be moved after “city.”

explanation: The phrase adds extra information about when the chapter was written and is properly punctuated.

prompt:
The variety of tools available to the conservators <u>allow</u> them to examine pigments without removing samples.
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: allow
choices.B: allows
choices.C: have allowed
choices.D: allowing

explanation: The singular subject “variety” takes the singular verb “allows.”

prompt:
A student wants to revise a sentence for precision:
“The policy changed the port.”
Which choice best improves the sentence by making it more precise?

choices.A: The policy changed the port.
choices.B: The policy affected many parts of the port.
choices.C: Within two years, the policy shortened cargo-truck lines at the gate, expanded weekend loading hours, and shifted more shipments to rail.
choices.D: The policy had several helpful effects.

explanation: This revision replaces a vague claim with specific, concrete effects.

prompt:
[1] In cloud forests, tree branches intercept moisture from low clouds. [2] That moisture can drip to the ground even when little rain falls. [3] Different slopes and elevations capture cloud water differently. [4] For that reason, conservation plans often treat nearby patches of forest as hydrologically distinct.
Which choice best describes the most logical placement for the following sentence?
“A ridge only a short distance away may therefore contribute very differently to local water supply.”

choices.A: Before sentence 1
choices.B: After sentence 2
choices.C: After sentence 3
choices.D: After sentence 4

explanation: The added sentence follows the discussion of variable cloud capture and leads into the conclusion about treating patches as distinct.

prompt:
The report originally described the mentoring program as “widely useful,” but that wording failed to show that the clearest gains appeared among first-year staff in field offices, while senior staff showed little measurable change. The editor suggests replacing “widely useful” with <u>most useful for first-year field staff</u>.
Which choice best describes the effect of the revision?

choices.A: It makes the statement more precise by specifying who benefited most.
choices.B: It makes the statement less formal by replacing a technical phrase with slang.
choices.C: It changes the meaning by claiming that senior staff were harmed by the program.
choices.D: It introduces an unsupported emotional judgment.

explanation: The revision identifies first-year field staff as the group showing the clearest gains.

prompt:
In an experiment, fish in tanks with a dark vertical stripe on one wall returned to a shelter more quickly after being startled than fish in identical tanks without the stripe. The researchers suggest that the stripe may have acted as an orientation landmark.
Which choice is best supported by the text?

choices.A: Visual landmarks may help fish orient themselves more quickly after a disturbance.
choices.B: The stripe made the shelter unnecessary for the fish.
choices.C: Fish can recognize landmarks only in deep water.
choices.D: The experiment proved that darker tanks always reduce stress.

explanation: Fish returned to shelter faster when the stripe was present, suggesting it served as a landmark.

prompt:
An essay on interview transcripts ends by noting that transcripts preserve words but not the pace, overlap, or hesitation of live speech. For that reason, oral historians often archive audio recordings alongside transcripts even when the written version is complete.
What is the function of the final sentence in the text?

choices.A: It applies the earlier idea to explain an archival practice.
choices.B: It narrows the discussion to the materials used to store recordings.
choices.C: It argues that written transcripts are unnecessary.
choices.D: It repeats the earlier claim without extending it.

explanation: The final sentence uses the point about what transcripts miss to explain why audio is archived alongside text.
